planting.note: Plant bare-root or container stock in early spring before bud break in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun; Damson plum is an ancient European plum type with smaller, more astringent, intensely flavored oval fruits that are outstanding for preserves, jams, gin, and damson cheese — it requires less winter chill than Japanese plums and is one of the hardiest and most disease-resistant plum types.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, deep soil in full sun; prune to open center form after harvest. Prunus domestica 'Damson' (Damson plum or Blue Damson) is an ancient, small-fruited European plum type — distinct from both Japanese plums (P. salicina) and common European plums in being smaller, more astringent, and more intensely flavored when raw, but superb when cooked or preserved. The small (1-inch), oval, blue-purple to nearly black fruits ripen in late summer through fall with a rich, complex, winelike, slightly tannic flavor that becomes sweet and luscious when cooked with sugar. Damson plum is the traditional fruit of English damson jam, damson cheese (a firm fruit paste), damson gin (sloe-gin equivalent made with damsons rather than sloes), and damson wine. The Shropshire Damson is the most celebrated English variety; Merryweather is the largest-fruited commercial type. Damson trees are notably hardy, long-lived, disease-resistant, and productive — often naturalizing at hedgerow edges across England — and require less winter chill than Japanese plums. Generally self-fertile or partially self-fertile; a pollination partner improves yields.
sources: University of Minnesota Extension — Stone Fruits

planting.note: Plant male and female vines in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in full sun to part shade; Arctic beauty kiwi is the hardiest of all kiwi species, surviving to -40°F (zone 3), and produces smooth-skinned, grape-sized kiwi fruits — the male plants are also ornamental, developing spectacular pink, white, and green variegated foliage.

directions: Plant male and female vines (one male per 6-8 females) on a strong trellis or pergola in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic soil; prune heavily each winter to manage size. Actinidia kolomikta (Arctic beauty kiwi or kolomikta kiwi) is an East Asian kiwi vine native to the cold, humid forests of Siberia, northern China, Korea, and Japan — the hardiest of all Actinidia species, surviving temperatures to -40°F (USDA zone 3) with no winter protection. It produces abundant, smooth-skinned, grape-sized fruits (no need to peel) with green flesh, a flavor similar to full-sized kiwi fruit, and a slightly sweeter, more complex character — excellent fresh or in fruit salads, smoothies, and preserves. The vine is dioecious — plant at least one male per 6-8 female vines for pollination and fruit set. What makes Arctic beauty kiwi especially garden-worthy is the extraordinary variegated foliage of the male plants: in late spring and early summer, the new leaves emerge with spectacular patches and splashes of bright pink, cream-white, and green — rivaling any ornamental vine in beauty. Female vines are solid green but highly productive. A superb ornamental-edible vine for cold-climate gardens where fuzzy kiwi (A. deliciosa) cannot survive.
sources: Cornell University Cooperative Extension — Kiwi Vine
University of Minnesota Extension — Hardy Kiwi

common_name: Jungle Flame (Flame of the Woods, West Indian Jasmine)

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, moist, humus-rich, acidic (pH 5.0–6.0), fertile soil in full sun to part shade; ixora is a cornerstone landscape shrub of South Florida, South Texas, and the Gulf Coast in zones 9–12, producing year-round clusters of vivid red, orange, pink, or yellow tubular flowers — one of the most continuously blooming tropical shrubs.

directions: Plant in well-drained, acidic (pH 5.0–6.0), humus-rich, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; acidify soil with sulfur if pH is above 6.0 — ixora is highly pH-sensitive and yellows rapidly in alkaline soil. Ixora coccinea (jungle flame or flame of the woods) is one of the most widely grown tropical landscape shrubs in South Florida, South Texas, Central America, and tropical Asia — prized for its dense, nearly year-round display of brilliant, dome-shaped flower clusters (corymbs) of small, tubular, four-petaled flowers in glowing shades of red, orange, salmon, pink, white, and yellow. Modern landscape cultivars include compact ('Nora Grant,' 'Super King,' 'Dwarf') and standard forms; the classic straight species produces vivid scarlet-red flowers. Ixora's critical cultural requirement is acidic soil (pH 5.0–6.0); in the alkaline soils common in coastal Florida and South Texas, plants develop interveinal chlorosis (yellowing between leaf veins due to iron and manganese deficiency) and decline unless soil is regularly acidified with sulfur or treated with chelated iron. The flowers are a nectar source for butterflies and hummingbirds; in traditional medicine (Ayurveda), various parts of the plant are used for a range of ailments. One of the most rewarding tropical shrubs where it is winter-hardy.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Ixora
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Tropical Shrubs
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Landscape Plants

common_name: Black Radish (Spanish Black Radish, Winter Radish)

planting.note: Direct sow 6–10 weeks before first fall frost in well-drained, deeply cultivated, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; black radish is a large, winter-storage radish with a dramatically pungent, horseradish-like flavor that mellows considerably when cooked — plant in fall for harvest before hard freezes.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1/2 inch deep in deeply cultivated, well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; thin to 4–6 inches for large root development. Raphanus sativus 'Black Spanish' (black radish or Spanish black radish) is a large, ancient European winter radish producing round to turnip-shaped roots with jet-black, rough, firm skin and strikingly white, dense, extremely pungent flesh — the flavor is intensely sharp, mustard-hot, and horseradish-like when raw, making it far more assertive than common spring radishes. Black radish is one of the oldest cultivated radish types — documented in ancient Egypt, Greece, and Rome — and remains popular in Eastern Europe, Germany, and France as a traditional winter vegetable. Raw, it is grated and dressed with salt, sour cream, or vinegar as a condiment (German Schwarzer Rettich); cooked, the pungency mellows considerably and the radish becomes milder and sweet. The roots store exceptionally well in cool conditions for weeks to months — a genuine winter storage vegetable unlike spring radishes, which must be used immediately. Black radish is also highly valued in naturopathic medicine for liver-supportive and digestive-stimulating properties attributed to high glucosinolate content.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Cool-Season Vegetables

common_name: Ruby Red Chard (Rhubarb Chard, Red-stalked Chard)

planting.note: Direct sow fall through early winter (September–November in zone 9) in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, moisture-retentive soil in full sun; Ruby Red chard is one of the most ornamental edibles for the vegetable garden — the brilliant crimson stems and red-veined, glossy dark-green leaves are as decorative as they are nutritious.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1/2 inch deep in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; thin to 6–9 inches; harvest outer leaves continuously. Beta vulgaris 'Ruby Red' (Ruby Red chard or Rhubarb chard) is a variety of Swiss chard selected for its dramatic, brilliant red stems (petioles) and red-veined dark-green to red-flushed leaves — making it simultaneously one of the most ornamental and one of the most nutritious crops in the vegetable garden. The vivid crimson stems get their color from betalain pigments (the same pigment group responsible for red beet color) which have antioxidant properties. The flavor is mild and slightly earthy, similar to spinach with a slightly stronger taste; the stems are firmer and need slightly longer cooking than the leaves. In zone 9, Ruby Red chard is a superb fall-through-spring crop, sown from September through November for harvest from November through May — it tolerates light frost and performs through the cool season until summer heat bolts it. The ornamental value is substantial: planted in rows or mixed borders, the glowing red stems create strong visual impact, making this one of the few vegetables that earns a place in ornamental beds. As with all chard, cut-and-come-again harvest extends production for months.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Swiss Chard
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Cool-Season Vegetables
North Carolina State Extension — Chard Production

common_name: Wood Rose (Yellow Morning Glory, Hawaiian Wood Rose)

planting.note: Direct sow or plant rooted cuttings in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy to loamy soil in full sun; wood rose is a vigorous tropical vine grown for its extraordinary, large, pale-yellow morning glory flowers and, uniquely, for the spectacular dried seed pods — the opened, star-shaped, woody calyces resemble carved wooden roses and are prized in dried floral arrangements.

directions: Direct sow scarified seeds in warm, well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; wood rose is drought-tolerant and vigorous once established — provide a very strong support. Merremia tuberosa (wood rose or Hawaiian wood rose) is a large, fast-growing tropical vine in the morning glory family (Convolvulaceae) native to Mexico and Central America, now naturalized and widely grown throughout the tropics. It produces large (3-inch), beautiful, pale golden-yellow, funnel-shaped morning glory flowers throughout the warm season. But the real treasure of wood rose is its extraordinary seed pod: as the flower fades and the fruit ripens, the calyx (the five-lobed green backing of the flower) expands and stiffens into a large, starfish-shaped, papery-woody structure 2–3 inches across, remaining on the vine to dry naturally. When fully dry, these calyces are deep chestnut-brown, rigid, and resemble intricately carved wooden roses — hence the name. The dried pods are highly prized in the craft and dried flower market, used in wreaths, arrangements, and floral crafts. Seed the pods during summer, collect in fall, and air-dry for use. In Hawaii, where the vine has naturalized widely, it is considered invasive in some native plant habitats. NOTE: Seeds may contain mildly psychoactive ergot alkaloids (similar to morning glory seeds) — do not ingest.
sources: University of Hawaii Cooperative Extension — Merremia tuberosa
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Invasive Vines
